ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class; no specific entry for honey-sweetened less-sugar variant. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers ready-to-drink green tea; no specific code for honey-sweetened reduced-sugar variant. Closest defensible match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for honey green tea beverage. Null used to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to a typical takeaway cup of honey green tea served in Singapore hawker centres and beverage stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Green Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Green Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010049. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value from International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; Green tea beverage GI analogy
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for honey green tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on: (1) honey GI ~55–58 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); (2) brewed green tea itself has negligible carbohydrate and GI ~0; (3) the beverage's glycaemic response is driven entirely by honey-derived sugars (fructose + glucose blend), yielding a blended GI of approximately 55. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 8.26 × 2.5) / 100 = 11.4 g, classified as medium GL. This is a conservative estimate; actual GI may vary with honey variety and dilution. Note: GI 55 is considered low according to the University of Sydney GI Database classification (low < 55, medium 56-69, high > 70).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 8.26 g sugar per 100 g (20.7 g per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Despite negligible sodium (1 mg/100 g), the 8.26 g/100 g sugar content classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage; regular SSB consumption independently elevates blood pressure and cardiovascular risk (AHA 2018), so individuals with hypertension should choose completely unsweetened green tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — 8.26 g sugar/100 g with negligible fibre (0.03 g/100 g); low GI ~55, medium GL ~11.4 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: This 'less sugar' honey green tea remains a sugar-sweetened beverage with 20.7 g of rapidly absorbed honey-derived sugars per 250 ml serving and virtually no fibre to blunt glycaemic response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid it and select genuinely unsweetened green tea (0 g sugar) to benefit from green tea polyphenols without blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar load from honey — 20.7 g per 250 ml serving; negligible saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The added sugar content (20.7 g per serving) exceeds the AHA daily added-sugar limit for women (25 g) in a single beverage; while fat and sodium are negligible, habitual consumption of this SSB contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and increased CVD risk, and unsweetened green tea is the preferred alternative for cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Green Tea (Less Sugar) is a popular ready-to-drink hot or cold beverage widely available at Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and bubble tea stalls. It is prepared by brewing green tea leaves or green tea bags in hot water and sweetening the infusion with a reduced quantity of honey compared to the standard recipe. The resulting drink has a light golden-green colour, a mild grassy and floral flavour from the green tea, and a gentle sweetness from the honey. Per 100 ml, it provides 43 kcal, 8.26 g of carbohydrate (entirely as sugar), 0.01 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g). Despite the 'less sugar' label, the beverage still contains 8.26 g of sugar per 100 g — equivalent to approximately 20.7 g of sugar in a standard 250 ml serving — which classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions. The sugar is derived from honey, which is a blend of fructose and glucose with a glycaemic index of approximately 55 (low). The glycaemic load per 250 ml serving is approximately 11.4 g, placing it in the medium GL range. Sodium is negligible at 1 mg/100 g. Green tea polyphenols (catechins, EGCG) are present in the brewed base and have documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; however, the added honey sugar largely offsets these benefits for individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, or cardiovascular disease. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its rapid glycaemic impact, and is a moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its SSB classification. Individuals seeking the health benefits of green tea should choose completely unsweetened green tea.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id D08010049, confidence 0.95). All per-100g macros and available micronutrients are preserved exactly from the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, clearly documented.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D08010049); this is consistent with a brewed tea beverage containing no fat-bearing ingredients. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same source. protein_g is 0.01 g/100 g (trace, not zero). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific honey green tea (less sugar) preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D08010049 as of 2026. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the approved GI database. The 'less sugar' designation refers to a reduced honey quantity relative to the standard honey green tea recipe; the beverage still qualifies as an SSB under HPB and WHO criteria (sugar > 5 g/100 g in a beverage). Ontology codes use approximate matches: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 (green tea beverage) and FoodEx2 A0FJP (green tea, ready to drink); no exact codes exist for the honey-sweetened less-sugar variant. GI 55 is classified as 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database criteria (low < 55, medium 56-69, high > 70).
